generated_oml:
// C3: Physical Sensing Components //
instance PrimaryMotorShaftEncoder : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Encoder directly coupled to motor shaft for high-precision rotational position and velocity measurement with 0.06mm repeatability and 0.1mm accuracy. Positioned at primary location for direct motion verification."
    DTDFVocab:producedData MotorShaftTelemetryData
]

instance SecondaryStageEncoder : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Encoder installed at secondary stage (gearbox output/linear actuator) for indirect position verification with 0.06mm repeatability and 0.1mm accuracy. Provides cross-verification of mechanical motion with primary encoder."
    DTDFVocab:producedData SecondaryStageTelemetryData
]

// C4: Physical-to-Virtual Interaction
instance MotorShaftTelemetryData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom PrimaryMotorShaftEncoder
    base:desc "Real-time sensor telemetry streamed via MQTT protocol containing rotational position, velocity, and derived motion parameters from primary motor shaft encoder."
]

instance SecondaryStageTelemetryData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom SecondaryStageEncoder
    base:desc "Real-time sensor telemetry streamed via MQTT protocol containing positional data from secondary stage encoder for cross-verification with primary encoder measurements."
]

// C10: Models/Data
instance SysMLFunctionalArchitectureModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "SysML Internal Block Diagram (IBD) representing component-connector architecture of the manufacturing system with functional decomposition and interface specifications."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SysMLModelingTool
]

instance MontiArcBehavioralModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "MontiArc Automaton-based behavioral model describing system state transitions and control logic using state machines for operational scenarios."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo MontiArcTool
]

instance SimulinkPhysicsModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "MathWorks Simulink model implementing physics-based simulation of mechanical dynamics, control systems, and operational behaviors for predictive analysis."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SimulinkSimulationTool
]

instance DigitalShadowDataTrace : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Aggregated contextual data trace combining real-time sensor measurements (position, velocity) and operational logs from physical system via MQTT protocol."
    DTDFVocab:fromData MotorShaftTelemetryData, SecondaryStageTelemetryData
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DataAggregationMiddleware
]

instance OperationalPerformanceMetrics : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Derived performance metrics including positional accuracy deviations, motion consistency analysis, and operational efficiency indicators from processed sensor data."
    DTDFVocab:fromData DigitalShadowDataTrace
]

// C11: Enablers
instance MQTTTelemetryProtocol : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Mosaic-based Message Queuing Telemetry Transport protocol enabling real-time, lightweight data transmission of sensor readings and live measurements from physical components to digital representation."
    DTDFVocab:enables DataAggregationService
]

instance SysMLModelingTool : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "SysML v1.7 compliant modeling tool supporting Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs) for component-connector architecture modeling and state machines for behavioral specification."
]

instance MontiArcTool : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "MontiArc architecture description language tool supporting component-connector models and automata-based behavioral description for system validation."
]

instance SimulinkSimulationTool : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "MathWorks Simulink environment for detailed behavioral modeling, simulation, and physics-based analysis of system dynamics and operational scenarios."
]

instance DataAggregationMiddleware : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Middleware component aggregating and abstracting raw sensor data traces into contextual, purpose-specific digital shadow representations for downstream analysis."
]

// C6: Services
instance DataAggregationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Service continuously collecting and structuring real-time sensor data from physical components into standardized digital shadow traces for monitoring and analysis."
    DTDFVocab:provides DigitalShadowTraceInsight
]

instance MonitoringDashboardService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Real-time dashboard service visualizing live operational metrics, sensor readings, and derived performance indicators for stakeholder monitoring."
    DTDFVocab:provides OperationalMonitoringInsight
]

instance BehavioralValidationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Service validating system behavior against specified requirements using SysML state machines and MontiArc automata for consistency checks."
    DTDFVocab:provides BehavioralConsistencyInsight
]

instance SimulationPredictionService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Service executing physics-based simulations in Simulink to predict system behavior under various operational conditions and scenarios."
    DTDFVocab:provides PredictiveBehaviorInsight
]

// C17: Insights/Actions
instance DigitalShadowTraceInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Aggregated contextual data trace representing the digital shadow of physical system operations, enabling evidence-based analysis and decision support."
]

instance OperationalMonitoringInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Real-time visualization of live operational parameters including positional accuracy, motion consistency, and system health indicators for stakeholder monitoring."
]

instance BehavioralConsistencyInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Validation results confirming alignment between specified behavioral requirements (state machines) and actual system operation through automated consistency checks."
]

instance PredictiveBehaviorInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Predictive analysis results from Simulink simulations showing expected system behavior under various operational scenarios and conditions."
]

// C1: Environment
instance ManufacturingSystemEnvironment : DTDFVocab:Environment [
    base:desc "Operational environment encompassing industrial manufacturing systems with complex engineered components, sensors, and actuators."
    base:contains PrimaryMotorShaftEncoder, SecondaryStageEncoder, SysMLFunctionalArchitectureModel, MontiArcBehavioralModel, SimulinkPhysicsModel, DigitalShadowDataTrace, OperationalPerformanceMetrics
]

// C2: Components
instance RobotAxisControlComponent : DTDFVocab:Component [
    base:desc "Modular control component managing motion control, positioning, and operational parameters for a single robot axis within the manufacturing system."
    base:isContainedIn ManufacturingSystemEnvironment
]

instance DigitalShadowAggregatorComponent : DTDFVocab:Component [
    base:desc "Component responsible for aggregating raw sensor data into structured digital shadow representations through middleware processing and abstraction."
    base:isContainedIn ManufacturingSystemEnvironment
    base:contains DataAggregationMiddleware
]

instance twinning_time_scale : DTDFVocab:TimeScale [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system operates within a **real-time, data-driven** temporal framework, where time series data from all sensors are continuously streamed via **MQTT** for synchronization between the physical robot axis (MABI MAX 100) and its digital representation. The system emphasizes **continuous, high-frequency data acquisition** to maintain an up-to-date digital model, though explicit synchronization frequencies (e.g., Hz or ms intervals) or latency requirements are not detailed in the provided documents. The temporal granularity is implicitly tied to the **sensor sampling rates** and **MQTT streaming throughput**, which are not quantified. No discrete-event or hybrid time-scale modeling approaches (e.g., event-driven vs. continuous) are referenced in the context of the Digital Twin’s temporal behavior."
]

instance dt_constellation : DTDFVocab:Constellation [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system’s orchestration and coordination are framed within the broader context of **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** and **architecture description languages (ADLs)**. The document highlights the use of **component & connector perspectives** (e.g., ACME, AADL, MontiArc, or SysML IBDs) to describe systems, which inherently supports **modular and composable architectures**. For Digital Twins, the **active components** (unlike passive Digital Shadows) must integrate **behavioral models** (e.g., state machines in SysML v1.7 or MontiArc Automaton) with simulation tools (e.g., Simulink) or general-purpose code (C++, Java). The **resource management** and **system-wide coordination** are implied to rely on **heterogeneous tool integration**, where different modeling notations and tools (e.g., SysML for architecture, Simulink for dynamics) must be synchronized. No explicit **orchestration patterns** (e.g., microservices, event-driven architectures, or service meshes) are mentioned, but the reference to **active components** suggests a **service-oriented or agent-based** approach may underpin the Digital Twin’s autonomy. The document does not specify **real-time coordination protocols** (e.g., DDS, MQTT, or gRPC) or **failure recovery mechanisms**."
]

instance life_cycle_stages : DTDFVocab:EvolutionStage [
    base:desc "
The lifecycle of a Digital Twin (DT) system is structured through **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, which decompose system modeling into hierarchical abstraction layers: **requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers**. Transition management between these stages is facilitated by **architecture description languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME, AADL, MontiArc, or SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)**, which define components, interfaces, connectors, and composition rules. Behavior modeling is further refined using **state machines in SysML v1.7** or **MontiArc Automaton**, while detailed simulations are integrated via tools like **MathWorks Simulink** or general-purpose languages (C++, Java). Lifecycle governance involves integrating heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives and ensuring consistency across **multiple modeling notations and tools**, as emphasized by methodologies like **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, or SMArDT**. The lifecycle transitions from abstract conceptual models to executable simulations, with governance frameworks ensuring traceability and compliance across phases.

**Key governance approaches** include:
- **Abstraction decomposition** via MBSE layers (requirements → functional → logical → physical).
- **Toolchain integration** for cross-notation consistency (e.g., SysML + Simulink).
- **Stakeholder alignment** through multi-perspective modeling.
"
]

instance fidelity_and_validity_considerations : DTDFVocab:FidelityConsideration [
    base:desc "
The Digital Twin system adheres to **fidelity and validity** requirements through structured methodologies and validation frameworks:

1. **Model Fidelity Requirements**:
   - **Abstraction Hierarchy**: Follows the **RFLP methodology** (Requirements → Functions → Logics → Physics) to ensure progressive refinement of models from high-level specifications to low-level physical implementations.
   - **Multi-Notation Consistency**: Models in **SysML**, **AADL**, and **simulation tools** (e.g., Simulink) must align to maintain **logical and physical fidelity** across abstraction layers.
   - **Behavioral Accuracy**: Behavioral models (state machines, automata) are validated against **simulation outputs** and **real-world data traces** to ensure consistency.

2. **Validation and Verification Methods**:
   - **Deviation Analysis**: Primary validation metric is the **comparison of simulated data versus measured data** (from physical sensors or Digital Shadow traces) to quantify discrepancies.
   - **Cross-Tool Validation**: Models are cross-verified across **MBSE tools** (e.g., SysML → Simulink) to detect inconsistencies in structural or behavioral representations.
   - **Uncertainty Quantification**: Sources of uncertainty include:
     - **Sensor Noise**: Encoder resolution limits and mechanical backlash (implied by 0.1 mm accuracy specs).
     - **Model Approximations**: Simplifications in behavioral models (e.g., lumped parameters in Simulink) vs. detailed physics-based simulations.
     - **Data Latency**: Delays in real-time data aggregation (not quantified but acknowledged in Digital Shadow contexts).

3. **Error Detection and Correction Mechanisms**:
   - **Anomaly Detection**: Implicit via **deviation thresholds** in simulated vs. measured data (e.g., flagging outliers in positional measurements).
   - **Feedback Loops**: Digital Twins may incorporate **corrective simulations** or **adaptive control** (e.g., PID tuning) to mitigate errors, though specific algorithms are **not documented**.
   - **Quality Assurance Processes**:
     - **Model Audits**: Periodic reviews of **SysML/AADL diagrams** for logical consistency.
     - **Simulation Benchmarking**: Validation of behavioral models against **gold-standard simulations** (e.g., high-fidelity physics engines).
     - **Data Sanitization**: Filtering of sensor traces to remove **spikes or outliers** before aggregation into the Digital Shadow.

4. **Uncertainty Reduction Goals**:
   - **Target Uncertainty**: Aimed at reducing **systematic errors** (e.g., encoder calibration drift) and **random errors** (e.g., sensor noise) to within **0.1 mm positional tolerance**.
   - **Closed-Loop Refinement**: Iterative updates to models based on **real-time feedback** from the Digital Shadow to improve long-term fidelity.
"
]

instance dt_technical_connection : DTDFVocab:TechnicalConnection [
    base:desc "
The **technical network infrastructure** for this Digital Twin system is not explicitly detailed in the provided documents. However, the document mentions **MQTT** as a communication protocol for data transmission from physical systems to digital representations (digital shadows). No other protocols (e.g., OPC UA, Modbus), network topologies (e.g., SOA, microservices), or standards (e.g., ISO 20243) are referenced. The document does not provide specifications for **bandwidth**, **latency**, **reliability mechanisms**, or **security protocols** beyond the implicit requirement for a functional data flow. The **Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl** system is mentioned as an interface for physical data, but no details on its integration with digital systems or network architecture are given.
"
]

instance dt_hosting_deployment : DTDFVocab:Deployment [
    base:desc "
The **deployment infrastructure** for this Digital Twin system is not explicitly described in the provided documents. The document references **MathWorks Simulink** and **SysML** for modeling and simulation, which are typically deployed on **general-purpose computing platforms** (e.g., on-premise servers or cloud environments), but no specific hosting platforms (e.g., AWS, Azure) or edge/on-premise deployment models are mentioned. **Computational and storage requirements** are not detailed, though the use of **simulation tools** implies resource-intensive environments. **Scalability mechanisms** are not addressed, nor are **deployment models** (e.g., Kubernetes, Docker) or **availability/reliability assurance** protocols. The document does not provide evidence for hardware (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson) or software (e.g., MongoDB) specifications beyond the modeling tools.
"
]

instance horizontal_integration : DTDFVocab:HoriIntegration [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system integrates with external systems via **MQTT-based streaming** of sensor time series, enabling real-time data exchange with other components or enterprise systems. While no explicit **interoperability standards** (e.g., OPC UA, ROS) or **legacy system wrappers** are mentioned, the **heterogeneous stakeholder integration** (Section II.A) suggests compatibility with diverse tools (e.g., simulation, MBSE, or ERP systems) through standardized protocols like MQTT. Dependencies on external systems are implied for **data aggregation and abstraction**, but specific integration patterns (e.g., model federation) are not detailed in the documents."
]

instance standardization : DTDFVocab:Standardization [
    base:desc "
The Digital Twin system adheres to **industry standards** primarily through the adoption of **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies** and **architecture description languages (ADLs)**. **Relevant standards** include:
- **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)** for system structure and behavior modeling (ISO/IEC 19512).
- **AADL (Architecture Analysis & Design Language)** for component-based system design (SAE AS5506).
- **ACME (Architecture Description Language)** for component-connector modeling.
- **MontiArc** for automaton-based behavioral specifications.
**Interoperability standards** are implied by the **integration of heterogeneous tools** (e.g., SysML + Simulink + C++/Java), though explicit protocols (e.g., OPC UA, ROS) are not mentioned. **Certification requirements** are not detailed, but compliance frameworks are inferred through **MBSE traceability** and **ADL-based validation**. The system’s standardization aligns with **manufacturing and systems engineering domains**, emphasizing **modularity, traceability, and toolchain interoperability**.

**Notable gaps**:
- No explicit mention of **OPC UA, ROS, or IoT protocols**.
- No **formal certification frameworks** (e.g., ISO 23247 for DTs).
"
]

instance security_and_safety_considerations : DTDFVocab:SecuritySafety [
    base:desc "
The Digital Twin system for the MABI robot (as referenced in [44]) incorporates physical safety mechanisms such as a **safety cell** that restricts the maximum working range of the robotic arm to **[-90°, 90°]**, ensuring operator protection within predefined boundaries. Sensor data streams (e.g., joint angles, force/torque feedback) are transmitted via **MQTT** (as implied in [42] for digital shadow integration), though explicit security protocols (e.g., TLS, OAuth) or authentication systems are not documented. The system lacks explicit mention of cybersecurity protocols, threat mitigation strategies, or fail-safe mechanisms beyond the physical safety cell. Risk assessments or compliance with safety-critical standards (e.g., ISO 13849, IEC 61508) are not addressed in the provided documents."
]
